html:
Hantech is not the semiconductor gas-panel net-cash story in the prior draft. The real company is better understood as a small Korean process-equipment exporter transitioning from domestic petrochemical cyclicality into North American LNG, high-margin aftermarket heat exchangers, cryogenic tanks, and green-energy/nuclear optionality.

At W36,750, market cap is about W409 billion. FY2025 revenue was W171.9 billion, operating profit W38.2 billion, net income W30.8 billion, and FCF W58.0 billion, with W55.5 billion net cash, or about W5,000 per share, using 11.12 million shares outstanding. That is not a cash-floor thesis, but it is a clean balance sheet attached to a business now earning 22% operating margins.

The variant view is that Hantech is being traded as a Korean “theme stock” tied to Alaska LNG, Iran reconstruction, SMR, ammonia, and nuclear headlines. The better thesis is simpler: real orders are arriving. In January 2026, Hantech won a Shintech Louisiana heat-exchanger contract worth W28.4 billion, 18.2% of 2024 sales. A Burns & McDonnell North America contract was revised to W35.7 billion, 22.9% of sales. Kiwoom’s April 2026 note estimated 2025 year-end backlog at W160 billion, with over 80% recognized in 2026, and forecast 2026 revenue of W191 billion and operating profit of W44.8 billion.

That makes the stock about 11x my 2026 EPS estimate near W3,300 and around 8x EV/EBIT, before giving credit for the 2027 order cycle. If 2026 new orders exceed W200 billion as Kiwoom expects, and North American LNG/tank work starts replacing older lower-margin domestic petrochemical jobs, Hantech can earn W4,200 to W4,800 EPS in 2027. On that number, today’s price is only 7.5x to 8.8x forward earnings for a company with net cash, high ROE, scarce qualification credentials, and improving export mix.

The upside torque comes from three places. First, shell-and-tube heat exchangers are not commodity widgets when they sit inside LNG, refinery, petrochemical, or cryogenic systems with demanding safety and delivery requirements. Second, North America appears to be Hantech’s highest-margin geography, so revenue growth should carry mix improvement. Third, the market is still valuing the company like a newly listed cyclical contractor, while the order book is beginning to look more like a qualified global EPC supplier network asset.

The risks are real. Foosung still controls roughly 64%, so float is tight and exchangeable-bond related selling has created an overhang. Project timing can slip. A few large customers can swing revenue. The stock can be dragged around by retail themes that have little to do with quarterly earnings. And the net cash balance is useful but not large enough to underwrite the whole equity.

Still, the setup is attractive because the market is punishing the stock after a theme-driven fade while the fundamental order evidence has improved. The right way to own Hantech is not as a liquidation value story. It is a forward-cycle earnings story where a W400 billion market cap may be buying W45 billion plus of EBIT in 2026 and potentially W60 billion plus in 2027.

Current price: W36,750.

Bear target: W28,000, down 24%. Orders slip, margins normalize toward the mid-teens, and the stock trades near 9x trough EPS plus net cash.

Base target: W62,000, up 69%. 2027 EPS reaches about W4,400 and the market assigns 13x earnings for a net-cash, export-led process-equipment supplier with visible LNG backlog.

Bull target: W85,000, up 131%. North American LNG, tanks, ammonia, and nuclear-related equipment stack together, EBIT approaches W70 billion, and Hantech re-rates toward 11x EV/EBIT as a scarce Korean energy-infrastructure equipment compounder.
